title: Tanium Threat Intel Feed Application
spl:
index=endpoint sourcetype="tanium:intel"
| stats latest(import_time) as it by feed_name
| where now()-it > 172800
description: Intel feed freshness keeps IOC hunts and blocks aligned with current campaigns.
value: Intel feed freshness keeps IOC hunts and blocks aligned with current campaigns.
implementation: Track intel feed imports into Tanium. Alert when feeds are >48h stale.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_tanium`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=tanium:intel`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track intel feed imports into Tanium. Alert when feeds are >48h stale.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=endpoint sourcetype="tanium:intel"
| stats latest(import_time) as it by feed_name
| where now()-it > 172800
```

Understanding this SPL

**Tanium Threat Intel Feed Application** — Intel feed freshness keeps IOC hunts and blocks aligned with current campaigns.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=tanium:intel`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_tanium`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: endpoint; **sourcetype**: tanium:intel. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=endpoint, sourcetype="tanium:intel". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by feed_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where now()-it > 172800` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (stale feeds), Single value.
knownFalsePositives: Legitimate use of dual-purpose administrative tools (e.g. PowerShell, SSH, cloud CLIs) during maintenance or deployment can match this pattern — correlate with change tickets, asset criticality and user role before escalating.
